text: clay streets and the sun yet hours from rising into its kiln. He who would finish the circle sat removed upon a lip of the ridge. That blood man. A mottled horsehide draped about his frame and he was the only one hatless among us. His long legs were tucked beneath his lap, his eye inscrutable in the moonlight. He had but the one eye and no name he shared with the likes of us. Dull company he was. But we needed him near. # The mountains had been no more than rough sentinels sharpening out of the dim distance toward us when we lost our first rider in the scrubland. Old Podrel the whisperer, whose vanishing made me the eldest of us. There had been drunken argument regarding the stars that night as we eleven settled around an old well. Cassie had quit her chair in the firmament, claimed Podrel, and Orion lofted not the lion’s pelt but hung instead from the beast’s mouth. Great Oso had trundled around the earth from our sight. Most of us held no truck with the heavens, I among them, yet soon we all would come to confess that the stars did appear changed in the spread sky, and diminished as well, as though retreating from their forsaken watchers. It was when the next morning Podrel’s blanket was empty that my heart began to soften against such foolishness. The land we crossed was still low, the trees few. An easier country than the Mexican desert that waited beyond the mountains. The old man couldn’t have wandered far. We set out in twos, wary of the once-peaceful Apache tribes who though slaughtered for years by the whites were still known to lash out at us darker cousins. But the day brought nothing but night and weariness and we camped to wait on the sun. Julo disappeared while we slept and his brother Ramiro was taken the following night, whether by desire to find his kin or by our pursuer we did not know. Our cook, Lupe the Younger, before the fourth dawn. Finally the leader of our band, steelfaced Mantalbo with his rifles a great X upon his back, took his contempt of our growing unease into the dark and did not return. We all read the stars after that. Their cold portents glared clearer. We bound ourselves together when we slept until after the blood man came. We were one day into the mountains when William spied him up a redoak tree staring down at us, his eye dripping blood into the fire. Soro was the first to stab up into the branches, but the man receded into the greater reach of the foliage, skittering backward up the trunk like some pale spider. An hour found us far from that tree and huddled together in the dark risking neither fire nor sound. Our wrists were again bound together. We’d have bet our purses that yonder was the thing taking us one by one in the nights. But at first light we saw that he’d slithered from the redoak and scented our trail. He reposed beside me and that black stone was his pillow. We fell upon him linked as we were like a chain of paper dolls, limbs tangled. When the shouts rang off into silence he smiled. “You do not see the stars?” he said, lying uninjured at his rest upon the ground, the one eye a milky yellow and beside it a ruined hole scooped of its meat. “They have taken your companions. I too seek El Porvenir. You will arrive there whole.” We were six and the blood man traveled alongside with his oath of safe passage. And though we neither trusted nor counted him among us, it was in his presence that we held our number. # “I still think he’s got Mantalbo and them stashed somewhere,” Sorrén, the youngest left of us, said. He cocked a thumb at the hatless man who sat gazing upon the city. “Probably ate them.” “Maybe so,” William said. His orange hair shone like flames escaping the coals. “I wish he’d conjure up a rabbit or two. Jerky’s giving me mouth sores.” The rest of us said nothing to court attention from that lone figure. Someone spat into the fire and another chased after it with a stick, prodding the embers into a spray of sparks. Each looked into the turning flames and prayed to his mode of Creation. “What are you in mind of, Gris?” Huedro said after a time. He looked at me and soon the others followed suit. “I’m not in mind of much,” I said. It was a lie but it served to pry open my curiosity. “Fact is that man needs talking to.” I had to will myself over to his perch, clearing my throat like a nervous young suitor. “The men reckon you’re a wizard,” I said. The words seemed to die in the air on their way to him. The blood man stared out across the desert and bade his silence. Two of his long fingers crept out from under the hide, plucked a tick or a clod of dirt from his lap. “We’re all thankful to still be around come sunrise,” I said after a minute, “but time is we learn—” “The men reckon I am a wizard.” He did not turn as he cut into my words, his voice flat as his polished stone or the desert under his scrutiny. “What is your reckoning, Guenard?” I stepped back and my creaky legs nearly spilled me. He’d used my birthname when no man had since as a young man the first silver threaded my beard like early ore. “I reckon you know things,” I told him. The blood man sat unmoving as an effigy and watched the night. At length his hand appeared again and swept across the land. “One would think El Porvenir thrives in its dust, so unaltered it appears.” “I’m glad of
